name: Entity Structure Optimization
description: Choosing the right business entity (LLC, S-Corp, C-Corp) can save thousands in self-employment and income taxes. S-Corps allow owners to split income between salary and distributions, reducing SE tax.
requirements: Consult with CPA; may require state filings and additional tax returns
example: A freelancer earning $150K switches to S-Corp, pays $80K salary and takes $70K as distribution, saving ~$10,700 in SE tax.
caveats: S-Corp salary must be 'reasonable compensation'. IRS scrutinizes low salaries. Additional payroll costs.

name: Home Office + Vehicle Combo
description: Combining home office and vehicle deductions maximizes savings. Your home office establishes your 'tax home,' making all trips to client sites deductible business mileage.
requirements: Dedicated home office space; vehicle used for business; mileage log
example: With a qualifying home office, your 30-mile daily commute to a client becomes 100% deductible business mileage at $0.70/mile.
caveats: Home office must meet exclusive use test. Keep contemporaneous mileage records.

name: Retirement Contribution Maximization
description: Maximize pre-tax retirement contributions across multiple account types. Self-employed individuals can contribute to both a SEP IRA and a Solo 401(k) for different businesses.
requirements: Earned income; age-based limits apply; contribution deadlines vary by plan type
example: A self-employed person maxes Solo 401(k) at $23,500 employee + 25% employer match on net SE income, deferring $40K+ in taxable income.
caveats: Contribution limits are aggregate across employers for 401(k). SEP IRA and Solo 401(k) cannot both be used for same business.

name: Income Shifting via S-Corp
description: S-Corp election allows business owners to reduce self-employment tax by paying themselves a reasonable salary and taking remaining profits as distributions not subject to SE tax.
requirements: S-Corp election filed with IRS; payroll system; reasonable salary determination
example: Owner earns $200K, pays $100K salary (15.3% SE tax) and takes $100K as distribution (0% SE tax), saving ~$15,300.
caveats: Must pay reasonable compensation. IRS can reclassify distributions as wages. Requires payroll tax filings.

name: Section 199A QBI Deduction
description: Qualified Business Income deduction allows pass-through business owners to deduct up to 20% of qualified business income from their taxable income.
requirements: Pass-through business income; income below thresholds for specified service trades
example: A consultant with $150K QBI deducts 20% = $30,000 from taxable income, saving $7,200 at 24% bracket.
caveats: Specified service trades (law, accounting, health, consulting) face income phase-outs. W-2 wage and capital limits apply above thresholds.

name: Augusta Rule (Section 280A)
description: Rent your home to your business for up to 14 days per year tax-free. The business deducts the rent, and you don't report the rental income on your personal return.
requirements: Must have a legitimate business entity separate from you; fair market rent; documented business purpose for each rental day
example: S-Corp rents owner's home for 12 board meetings at $1,500/day = $18,000 deduction for the corp, tax-free income for the owner.
caveats: Must charge fair market value. Document each business use day. IRS scrutinizes excessive amounts. Get a rental comparable study.

name: Hire Your Children
description: Employ your children under 18 in your sole proprietorship. Wages are deductible to the business and may be tax-free to the child up to the standard deduction amount.
requirements: Child must perform legitimate work; age-appropriate tasks; reasonable pay for work performed
example: Pay your 15-year-old $14,600 for office work. Fully deductible to you, zero tax to child (under standard deduction), no FICA if sole prop.
caveats: Only works for sole proprietorships and husband-wife partnerships for FICA exemption. Must be real work at reasonable rates. Keep time records.

name: Tax Loss Harvesting
description: Sell investments at a loss to offset capital gains and up to $3,000 of ordinary income. Immediately reinvest in similar (not substantially identical) assets.
requirements: Taxable investment account; unrealized losses; understanding of wash sale rules
example: Sell stock with $15K loss, offset $12K in capital gains and deduct $3K against income. Reinvest in similar ETF immediately.
caveats: Wash sale rule: cannot buy substantially identical security within 30 days before or after. Losses carry forward indefinitely.
irs_reference: IRC §1091 (wash sale); IRC §1211; Schedule D; Form 8949

name: Backdoor Roth IRA
description: High earners can contribute to a Roth IRA indirectly by making a non-deductible traditional IRA contribution and immediately converting it. Provides tax-free growth.
requirements: No existing pre-tax IRA balances (to avoid pro-rata rule); income above Roth contribution limits
example: Contribute $7,000 non-deductible to traditional IRA, convert to Roth next day. Pay minimal tax on any gains during conversion.
caveats: Pro-rata rule makes this expensive if you have existing traditional IRA balances. Consider rolling pre-tax IRAs into 401(k) first.

name: Cost Segregation Study
description: Accelerate depreciation on commercial or rental property by reclassifying building components into shorter-lived asset categories (5, 7, or 15 years vs 27.5/39 years).
requirements: Own commercial or residential rental property; hire qualified cost segregation firm; property typically valued over $500K
example: On a $1M commercial building, cost seg reclassifies $300K to 5/7/15-year property, generating $80K+ in first-year bonus depreciation.
caveats: Can trigger depreciation recapture on sale. Study costs $5K-$15K. Most beneficial for properties held long-term. Consult tax advisor.
irs_reference: IRC §168; IRS Audit Technique Guide (Cost Segregation); Form 4562

name: Real Estate Professional Status
description: Qualifying as a real estate professional allows you to deduct rental losses against ordinary income without the $25,000 passive activity loss limitation.
requirements: 750+ hours in real estate activities; more than half of working time in real estate; material participation in each rental
example: RE professional with $80K in rental depreciation losses can deduct all of it against W-2 or business income instead of carrying forward.
caveats: IRS heavily audits RE professional status. Keep detailed contemporaneous time logs. Spouse's time doesn't count toward your hours.

name: HSA Triple Tax Advantage
description: Health Savings Accounts offer a triple tax benefit: contributions are tax-deductible, growth is tax-free, and qualified medical withdrawals are tax-free.
requirements: Enrolled in a High Deductible Health Plan (HDHP); not enrolled in Medicare; not claimed as dependent
example: Family contributes max $8,550 to HSA, saves ~$2,500 in taxes at 30% bracket. Invests and lets it grow tax-free for decades.
caveats: Must have HDHP. Over-contributions face 6% penalty. Keep receipts for medical expenses indefinitely for future reimbursement.

name: Charitable Giving with Appreciated Stock
description: Donate appreciated stock held over 1 year directly to charity. Deduct the full market value and avoid capital gains tax on the appreciation entirely.
requirements: Stock held over 1 year; donated to qualified 501(c)(3); deduction limited to 30% of AGI
example: Donate $10K of stock with $3K cost basis. Deduct $10K and avoid $1,400 in capital gains tax (20% rate). Total benefit: $3,900 at 25% bracket.
caveats: 30% AGI limit (vs 60% for cash). Must be held over 1 year. Get qualified appraisal for non-publicly-traded property over $5K.

name: Donor-Advised Fund Bunching
description: Bunch multiple years of charitable donations into a single year using a Donor-Advised Fund to exceed the standard deduction, then take standard deduction in off years.
requirements: Charitable giving history; itemizable in bunching years; DAF account
example: Instead of $5K/year for 3 years, donate $15K in year 1 to DAF (itemize), take standard deduction years 2-3. Net tax savings ~$1,500.
caveats: DAF contributions are irrevocable. You advise on grants but don't control the fund. Some DAFs have minimums and fees.

name: Section 179 First-Year Expensing
description: Deduct the full cost of qualifying business equipment and software in the year of purchase instead of depreciating over multiple years. 2025 limit is $1,250,000.
requirements: Tangible personal property used >50% for business; purchased (not leased) and placed in service during tax year
example: Buy $30K in equipment. Section 179 lets you deduct all $30K this year instead of $6K/year over 5 years. Tax savings: $7,200 at 24%.
caveats: Phase-out begins at $3,130,000 in total purchases. Cannot create or increase a net operating loss. SUV limit of $30,500.

name: Accountable Plan for Employee Expenses
description: S-Corp or C-Corp sets up an accountable plan to reimburse employee-shareholders for business expenses. Reimbursements are tax-free to the employee and deductible by the corp.
requirements: Written accountable plan; business connection; adequate accounting; return of excess amounts
example: S-Corp reimburses owner for $8K in home office, cell phone, internet, and mileage. Tax-free to owner, deductible to corp.
caveats: Must have written plan. Employee must substantiate expenses within 60 days and return excess within 120 days.

name: Maximize Education Credits
description: The American Opportunity Credit ($2,500) and Lifetime Learning Credit ($2,000) provide dollar-for-dollar tax reduction for education expenses.
requirements: Enrolled in eligible educational institution; income within limits; Form 1098-T
example: Pay $4K tuition for dependent. Claim $2,500 American Opportunity Credit (40% refundable = $1,000 even with no tax liability).
caveats: AOC limited to first 4 years of post-secondary. LLC is non-refundable. Income phase-outs apply. Cannot claim both for same student.

name: Qualified Business Income Stacking
description: Structure multiple businesses or income streams to maximize the Section 199A QBI deduction across different entities and income types.
requirements: Multiple business activities or income streams; understanding of aggregation rules
example: Separate consulting (SSTB) from product sales (non-SSTB) into different entities. Non-SSTB business gets full 199A deduction regardless of income.
caveats: Aggregation rules are complex. Anti-abuse rules prevent gaming. Specified service trade limitations still apply per activity.

name: Strategic Estimated Tax Payments
description: Time your Q4 state estimated tax payment in December vs January to control which year you claim the SALT deduction, maximizing the $10,000 cap benefit.
requirements: State income tax liability; itemizing deductions; monitoring SALT cap usage
example: If you're under the $10K SALT cap, pay Q4 state estimate in December to claim it this year. If at the cap, defer to January.
caveats: $10,000 SALT cap limits benefit. Don't prepay state tax if already at cap. AMT implications possible.

name: Net Operating Loss Carryforward
description: Business losses exceeding income create a Net Operating Loss that can be carried forward indefinitely to offset up to 80% of taxable income in future years.
requirements: Business loss exceeding other income; proper NOL computation; track carryforward amounts
example: Year 1: $50K business loss, $30K W-2 income. $20K NOL carries forward. Year 2: Apply $20K NOL against income, saving $4,800 at 24%.
caveats: 80% of taxable income limit for post-2017 NOLs. No carryback allowed (except farming). Track NOL schedule carefully.

name: Health Insurance Premium Deduction (Self-Employed)
description: Self-employed individuals can deduct 100% of health, dental, and long-term care insurance premiums as an above-the-line deduction, reducing AGI.
requirements: Self-employed with net profit; not eligible for employer-sponsored plan through spouse
example: Self-employed person pays $12,000/year for family health insurance. Full above-the-line deduction saves $2,880 at 24% bracket.
caveats: Cannot exceed net self-employment income. Cannot deduct if eligible for employer plan (including spouse's). Reduces AGI but not SE tax.

name: Bonus Depreciation on Vehicles
description: Heavy SUVs and trucks (GVWR over 6,000 lbs) qualify for bonus depreciation up to $30,500 in the first year under Section 179, plus additional bonus depreciation.
requirements: Vehicle GVWR over 6,000 lbs; used >50% for business; purchased new or used; placed in service during tax year
example: Buy $65K SUV (6,100 lb GVWR) used 80% for business. Section 179: $30,500 × 80% = $24,400 first-year deduction.
caveats: Must be used >50% for business. $30,500 Section 179 limit for SUVs. Luxury auto limits apply to lighter vehicles. Keep mileage log.

name: Qualified Opportunity Zone Investment
description: Invest capital gains into Qualified Opportunity Zone Funds to defer and potentially reduce capital gains taxes, with tax-free growth if held 10+ years.
requirements: Realized capital gains; investment in certified QOZ Fund within 180 days; long-term hold commitment
example: Invest $100K capital gain into QOZ Fund. Defer gain until 2026. Hold 10+ years and pay zero tax on QOZ Fund appreciation.
caveats: Original gain deferral ends in 2026 regardless. No more basis step-up for 5/7 year holds (expired 2026). High risk real estate investments.

name: Roth Conversion Ladder
description: In low-income years, strategically convert traditional IRA/401(k) funds to Roth, paying tax at a lower bracket now to avoid higher taxes later or in RMD years.
requirements: Traditional IRA or 401(k) balance; low-income year (sabbatical, retirement, business downturn)
example: In a year earning $40K (12% bracket), convert $50K from traditional to Roth. Pay $6K tax now instead of $12K+ at 24% in retirement.
caveats: Conversions are taxable income. Can push you into higher brackets. Medicare IRMAA surcharges if over thresholds. No undo (recharacterization).

name: Business Meal Deduction Strategy
description: Business meals with clients, prospects, or business associates are 50% deductible. Proper documentation turns everyday meals into legitimate deductions.
requirements: Business discussion before, during, or after meal; receipt with date, amount, attendees, business purpose
example: 20 client lunches at avg $80 = $1,600. 50% deductible = $800 deduction. At 24% bracket, saves $192.
caveats: Must have business discussion (not just goodwill). Keep receipts with who/what/when/why. Entertainment is NOT deductible (only the meal portion).

name: Home Sale Exclusion Planning
description: Exclude up to $250K ($500K married) of capital gain from the sale of your primary residence if you've lived there 2 of the last 5 years.
requirements: Owned and used as primary residence for 2 of last 5 years; haven't used exclusion in last 2 years
example: Married couple sells home with $400K gain. Entire gain excluded from taxation, saving $60K+ in capital gains tax.
caveats: Partial exclusion if moved for work, health, or unforeseen circumstances. Home office depreciation after 2009 is recaptured.

name: Maximize Dependent Credits
description: Stack child tax credit, dependent care FSA, and child/dependent care credit for maximum tax savings. Each has different rules and can often be combined.
requirements: Qualifying children under 17 (CTC) or under 13 (care credit); earned income; daycare expenses
example: 2 kids: $4,000 CTC + $5,000 dependent care FSA (saves $1,500 in tax) + remaining expenses toward care credit. Total: $5,500+ savings.
caveats: CTC phases out above $200K ($400K MFJ). Care credit reduces as income rises. FSA is use-it-or-lose-it.

name: Energy Efficiency Tax Credits
description: Residential clean energy credits cover 30% of solar panels, battery storage, heat pumps, and other energy improvements with no annual cap through 2032.
requirements: Install qualifying energy equipment in your primary or secondary residence; keep manufacturer certification
example: Install $25K solar system. 30% credit = $7,500 directly off your tax bill. Carry forward unused credit to future years.
caveats: Must be your residence (not rental). Credit is non-refundable but carries forward. Energy efficient home improvement credit capped at $3,200/year for non-solar items.

name: Maximize Moving Deductions (Military)
description: Active duty military members can deduct unreimbursed moving expenses for permanent station changes. This is one of the few remaining moving deductions.
requirements: Active duty military; permanent change of station orders; expenses not reimbursed by military
example: PCS move costs $8K, military reimburses $5K. Deduct remaining $3K above-the-line. At 22% bracket, saves $660.
caveats: Only available to active duty military. Civilian moving deduction was suspended 2018-2025 by TCJA.

name: 1031 Like-Kind Exchange
description: Defer capital gains tax by exchanging one investment or business property for another of like-kind. All real property is like-kind to other real property.
requirements: Qualified intermediary; 45-day identification window; 180-day closing deadline; both properties must be held for investment or business use
example: Sell a $500K rental property with $200K gain. Exchange into a $600K property via 1031 — defer the entire $200K gain ($40K+ in taxes).
caveats: Cannot be used for primary residences. Strict timelines (45/180 days). Boot (cash or non-like-kind property received) is taxable. Depreciation recapture deferred, not eliminated.

name: Conservation Easement Donation
description: Donate a conservation easement on land you own to a qualified organization. The easement permanently restricts development, and you receive a charitable deduction for the value of the restriction.
requirements: Land with conservation value (open space, habitat, historic); qualified appraisal; donation to qualified land trust or government; permanent restriction
example: Owner of 100-acre rural property donates development rights via conservation easement valued at $300K. Claims charitable deduction over 16 years.
caveats: IRS heavily scrutinizes syndicated conservation easements. Appraisal must meet strict standards. Deduction limited to 50% AGI (or 100% for qualified farmers/ranchers). Carryforward up to 15 years.

name: Cost Segregation for Real Estate
description: Engineering-based study that reclassifies building components into shorter depreciation periods (5, 7, or 15 years) to accelerate deductions on commercial or rental property.
requirements: Own depreciable real property; engage qualified cost segregation firm; property generally valued over $500K for cost-effectiveness
example: Cost seg on a $2M apartment building reclassifies $500K of components to 5/15-year life. Combined with bonus depreciation, generates $400K+ in first-year deductions.
caveats: Depreciation recapture (§1245/§1250) on sale. Study costs $5K-$15K. IRS Audit Technique Guide sets standards. Look-back studies available for existing properties.
irs_reference: IRC §168; IRS Audit Technique Guide (Cost Segregation); Form 4562

name: Opportunity Zone Investment (QOZ)
description: Invest capital gains into a Qualified Opportunity Fund that deploys capital in designated low-income census tracts. Defer original gains and potentially eliminate taxes on new appreciation.
requirements: Realized capital gains; investment in self-certified QOF within 180 days; fund must hold 90%+ assets in QOZ property
example: Invest $200K in capital gains into QOF that develops affordable housing in QOZ. Defer gain until 2026. After 10 years, all QOF appreciation is tax-free.
caveats: Deferred gain recognition in 2026. No longer any basis step-up for 5/7-year holds. Must file Form 8996 annually. Real estate development risk.

name: Agricultural Use Reclassification
description: Reclassify land for agricultural use to receive significantly lower property tax assessments. Many states offer ag-use valuation that reduces property taxes by 50-90%.
requirements: Land meeting state acreage minimums (typically 5-10 acres); bona fide agricultural activity (crops, livestock, timber); application to county assessor
example: 40-acre property assessed at $400K residential ($8K/year tax) is reclassified as agricultural ($40K assessment, $800/year tax) — saves $7,200/year.
caveats: State-specific rules vary widely. Rollback taxes if you change use within 5-10 years. Must maintain genuine agricultural activity. Annual reporting may be required.
irs_reference: IRC §2032A (special use valuation); state assessment statutes

name: Real Estate Professional Status (REPS)
description: Qualifying as a real estate professional allows rental activities to be treated as non-passive, enabling unlimited deduction of rental losses against other income.
requirements: 750+ hours in real estate trades/businesses; more than half of personal services in RE activities; material participation in each rental (or group election)
example: Full-time RE agent with $120K income and $80K in rental depreciation losses deducts all losses against active income, saving $19,200 at 24% bracket.
caveats: IRS heavily audits REPS claims. Must keep contemporaneous time logs. Both spouses cannot aggregate hours. Consider grouping election under §1.469-9(g).

name: Passive Activity Loss Rules
description: Understand and strategically manage passive activity losses. Active participants in rental real estate can deduct up to $25,000 in losses against non-passive income.
requirements: Active participation in rental activity; modified AGI below $150,000 for full benefit; losses from passive activities
example: Active landlord with $100K AGI and $25K rental loss deducts full $25K against W-2 income, saving $6,000 at 24% bracket.
caveats: $25K allowance phases out between $100K-$150K MAGI. Suspended losses carry forward. Released on full disposition of activity.

name: Rental Property Depreciation (27.5-Year)
description: Deduct the cost of residential rental buildings over 27.5 years using straight-line depreciation. Land is not depreciable, so allocate purchase price between building and land.
requirements: Own residential rental property placed in service; determine depreciable basis (exclude land); maintain depreciation schedule
example: Buy $300K rental (land $60K, building $240K). Annual depreciation: $240K / 27.5 = $8,727. At 24% bracket, saves $2,094/year in taxes.
caveats: Depreciation recapture taxed at 25% on sale (§1250). Land is never depreciable. Must begin depreciation when property is placed in service.

name: Section 121 Primary Residence Exclusion
description: Exclude up to $250,000 ($500,000 married filing jointly) of gain from the sale of your primary residence from capital gains tax.
requirements: Owned and used home as primary residence for 2 of last 5 years; not used exclusion in past 2 years
example: Married couple sells home for $800K (basis $350K). $450K gain, fully excluded under §121. Zero capital gains tax owed.
caveats: Partial exclusion for unforeseen circumstances (job change, health). Depreciation after May 6, 1997 is recaptured. Non-qualified use periods reduce exclusion.

name: Installment Sale Reporting
description: Spread capital gain recognition over multiple years by receiving sale proceeds in installments. Reduces the tax hit of a large gain in a single year.
requirements: At least one payment received after the tax year of sale; seller-financed note; not available for publicly traded securities
example: Sell $1M property with $400K gain on 5-year installment. Report $80K gain/year instead of $400K in year 1 — stay in lower brackets each year.
caveats: Depreciation recapture must be recognized in year of sale regardless. Interest on deferred tax applies above $5M in installment obligations. Related party rules apply.

name: Historic Rehabilitation Tax Credit
description: 20% federal tax credit for qualified rehabilitation expenses on certified historic structures. Encourages preservation while providing significant tax benefits.
requirements: Certified historic structure (NPS listed or in historic district); substantial rehabilitation test (expenses exceed adjusted basis); NPS Part 1-3 approval
example: Rehabilitate a historic commercial building for $500K in qualified expenses. 20% credit = $100K, claimed over 5 years ($20K/year).
caveats: Credit claimed ratably over 5 years (not all at once). Must reduce depreciable basis by credit amount. Building must be depreciable. Recapture if disposed within 5 years.

name: Low-Income Housing Tax Credit (LIHTC)
description: Federal tax credit for investors in qualified affordable housing projects. Credits are allocated by state housing agencies and provide dollar-for-dollar tax reductions over 10 years.
requirements: Investment in qualified LIHTC partnership/project; state housing agency allocation; 15-year compliance period; rent and income restrictions on tenants
example: Invest $200K in LIHTC syndication. Receive ~$180K in federal tax credits over 10 years plus depreciation deductions and potential residual value.
caveats: 15-year compliance period with strict rules. Recapture if noncompliance. Passive activity rules apply (need passive income or RE professional status). Complex partnership structures.

name: Timber & Natural Resource Depletion
description: Deduct the cost of timber, minerals, or other natural resources as they are harvested or extracted through depletion allowances. Timber can also qualify for capital gains treatment.
requirements: Ownership interest in timber, mineral, or natural resource property; established depletion basis; proper identification of timber volume or mineral reserves
example: Sell $100K of timber from 200 acres. With $30K depletion basis and §631(b) election, gain is taxed at long-term capital gains rate (15-20%) instead of ordinary rates.
caveats: Must establish depletion basis with professional timber cruise or mineral survey. Percentage depletion limited for certain minerals. Reforestation costs get amortization deduction.
irs_reference: IRC §611-§613; IRC §631; Form T (Timber); Schedule C or E

name: Oil & Gas Intangible Drilling Costs
description: Deduct intangible drilling costs (IDCs) — typically 65-85% of total well costs — in the year incurred. Working interest owners can also avoid passive activity limitations.
requirements: Working interest in oil or gas well; election to deduct IDCs currently (vs capitalize); AMT preference item consideration
example: Invest $100K in oil well drilling program. $75K in IDCs deducted immediately. At 37% bracket, saves $27,750 in year 1, plus ongoing depletion.
caveats: High economic risk (dry holes). IDCs are an AMT preference item. Working interest exception from passive rules requires unlimited liability. Percentage depletion has income limits.

name: Qualified Intermediary / Reverse 1031 Exchange
description: Use a reverse 1031 exchange to acquire replacement property before selling your relinquished property. An Exchange Accommodation Titleholder (EAT) holds the new property during the exchange period.
requirements: Qualified intermediary and EAT; 45-day identification + 180-day completion; both properties held for investment or business; cannot hold both simultaneously for more than 180 days
example: Find ideal replacement property before selling current rental. EAT acquires it for $800K. Sell old property 3 months later. Complete reverse 1031 exchange, deferring $150K in gains.
caveats: More expensive than forward exchange (EAT fees, carrying costs). Strict 180-day window. Additional financing complexity. Must follow Rev Proc 2000-37 safe harbor exactly.
